title: The Cold Start Problem
concept: The cold start problem occurs when a recommendation system has no historical data to work with — either for a brand-new user with no history, or a brand-new item with no views or ratings.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main data source collaborative filtering requires?

options.text: A grid of user-item interactions (clicks, ratings, purchases)
A rich database of item metadata and description tags

exercises.explain: Exactly! Collaborative filtering requires user-item interaction history to discover similarities in taste.

cards.title: The empty store
cards.body: Imagine opening a brand-new boutique. No customers have ever walked in, and you've just placed a brand-new designer shirt on the rack. A customer walks in for the first time. How do you recommend something to them? You know nothing about their taste, and you know nothing about who else liked that shirt. That's the cold start problem.

cards.title: Two sides of the cold start
cards.body: There are two types of cold starts: New User (we don't know their preferences yet) and New Item (we don't have user interaction history for it yet). Collaborative filtering fails completely on both, as it relies entirely on historical behavior data.

cards.title: A blank questionnaire
cards.body: To solve a New User cold start, streaming apps don't guess. They act like a host at a party: 'Choose three genres you love!' This immediate feedback seeds your profile, turning a cold user into a warm one. For a New Item cold start, content-based filtering helps by matching the new item's tags to existing profiles right away.

cards.title: How real apps break the ice
cards.body: When you sign up for a movie app, it asks you to pick a few movies you like (solving New User). When a creator uploads a new video, the platform uses content-based filtering to suggest it to users who like that genre (solving New Item), gathering the initial clicks needed for collaborative models later.

cards.title: You don't have to choose just one model
cards.body: It's a myth that apps must choose between content-based or collaborative filtering. Real-world systems are almost always hybrid systems — combining both approaches. They use content-based filtering to handle new items and new users, then transition to collaborative filtering once behavior data grows.

cards.title: Solving the Cold Start
cards.body: Strategies for warming up cold data:

left.text:
Show popular/trending items
Ask for genre preferences
Use demographic defaults

right.text:
Use content-based tags
Recommend to similar-profile users
Promote randomly to collect data

exercises.prompt: A music streaming app has just uploaded a brand-new song that has zero plays. Which type of cold start problem is this?

options.text: Neither — this is a validation split problem

exercises.explain: Correct! A newly uploaded song has zero play history, making it a New Item cold start.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Collaborative filtering can easily recommend a brand-new item that has never received any user ratings or views.
exercises.explain: False! Collaborative filtering relies on user interaction history. A brand-new item with zero interactions cannot be recommended by collaborative filtering.

exercises.sentence: Real-world apps build ___ recommendation systems, combining content-based and collaborative filtering to solve the cold start problem.
exercises.explain: Hybrid recommendation systems combine both strategies to leverage the strengths of each.

exercises.prompt: Which strategy is used to solve a New User cold start?

options.text: Asking the user to select their favorite genres and artists during sign-up
Waiting until the user has bought 50 products before showing any recommendations
Displaying only the oldest items in the database

exercises.explain: Correct! Asking for preferences during onboarding creates an initial user profile, resolving the New User cold start.

exercises.prompt: A new video streaming platform launches with zero user history. To solve the cold start problem, they design a system that: 1. Asks users for favorite genres on sign-up, and 2. Recommends newly uploaded videos by matching their genre tags directly to those signed-up user profiles. How are they solving the cold start?

options.text: Using content-based filtering to solve both the New User cold start (genres on sign-up) and New Item cold start (matching item tags directly to user profiles)
Using collaborative filtering, since user profile tags are a form of collaborative behavior
They are not solving the cold start problem

exercises.explain: Exactly! By asking for genres, they build a basic user profile without needing behavior history. By matching video tags to those profiles, they can recommend brand-new videos. Both steps use content-based filtering to overcome the lack of collaborative data.
